one_line_takeaway: A black-box RAG poisoning attack that unifies retrieval, generation, and concealment objectives via multi-LLM cross-model rewriting, matching white-box success (>90% ASR@5) while producing fluent, low-perplexity poisons that slip past perplexity and duplicate-text defenses—and it works on Alibaba's live BaiLian RAG.
key_finding: CPA-RAG achieves >90% attack success at top-k=5, effectively matching white-box PoisonedRAG while operating fully black-box, and keeps roughly a 5 percentage-point ASR advantage over black-box PoisonedRAG across top-k values. On NQ (GPT-4o/Contriever) it reaches ASR@5=0.92 and CASR=0.85 vs PoisonedRAG's 0.83/0.76 and Prompt Injection's 0.80/0.77. Its decisive edge is concealment: perplexity 43.01 (vs 487.77 for white-box PoisonedRAG and 63.21 for black-box), grammar/spelling errors 0.59 (vs 6.25 / 3.17), letting it evade perplexity and duplicate-text defenses that collapse other attacks—paper reports a 14.5-pp advantage over black-box baselines under the studied defenses. Multi-model generation is load-bearing: single-model variants transfer poorly (e.g. Qwen-only gets 0.94 on Qwen but 0.51 on GPT-4o), while Full reaches mean ASR ~0.94 at k=5. It also compromises the live Alibaba BaiLian commercial RAG system.
core_contribution: CPA-RAG is a black-box knowledge-base poisoning framework against Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. Its own novel contribution over prior work (esp. PoisonedRAG) is to (1) formalize three conditions an effective RAG-poisoning text must satisfy simultaneously—Retriever (the poison must be retrieved), Generation (the retrieved poison must induce a chosen target answer), and Concealment (the poison must be linguistically natural and survive mixed-document retrieval); and (2) generate poisons that satisfy all three as a single unified optimization rather than concatenating separate 'retrieval' and 'generation' fragments the way PoisonedRAG does. Mechanistically the paper's contribution is a three-stage pipeline: information collection (optional probing of retriever/LLM type), prompt-based initialization of fluent target-answer-inducing candidates across heterogeneous LLMs (GPT-4o, Claude, Qwen, DeepSeek), and iterative optimization that (a) rewrites candidates for retriever alignment scored by a hybrid open-source-retriever similarity evaluator (Contriever+ANCE+DPR, equally weighted) and (b) applies cross-model / cross-prompt refinement to boost concealment and reduce repetition. Note: PoisonedRAG, Paradox, CtrlRAG, Hotflip/GCG etc. are cited baselines/related work, NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Attacker chooses M target questions, each with a desired (incorrect) target answer, and wants the RAG system to emit that answer for those queries WITHOUT modifying the underlying model. Capability: the attacker cannot directly access the knowledge base but can inject adversarial documents into publicly editable/ingestible sources (forums, blogs, web content later crawled into the corpus). This is a preemptive black-box poisoning setting—no access to retriever parameters, LLM weights, or gradients. Two sub-cases: partial knowledge (retriever/LLM type inferred via probing) or zero internal knowledge relying only on public tools. By default N=5 adversarial texts are injected per target query. Explicitly distinguished from white-box gradient attacks (Hotflip/GCG) which the paper argues are unrealistic, and from CtrlRAG which needs repeated live querying of the target system.
limitations: Attack-side paper, so 'limitations' are mostly scope/threat caveats: (1) success depends on injected poisons actually being ingested into the target corpus and retrieved—real crawl/ingestion friction is not modeled beyond assuming editable public sources. (2) Concealment metrics like Flesch/FOG are argued 'up = better' (more complex text = harder to detect), which is a debatable proxy for human-undetectability; no human study. (3) TES ('Toxicity Efficiency Score') is a custom metric defined only in the appendix, complicating external comparison. (4) Evaluated defenses are the standard four; adaptive/RAG-specific defenses that target the multi-model fluency signature are not tested. (5) Generation relies on multiple strong commercial LLMs (GPT-4o, Claude, Qwen-Max, DeepSeek) as the attacker's toolkit—cost and API-policy friction are not discussed. (6) BaiLian result is a case demonstration; scale/robustness of the real-world compromise is limited to Appendix C detail.

quantitative_results: NQ (GPT-4o/Contriever): CPA-RAG ASR@5=0.92, F1@5=0.95, ASR@10=0.81, CASR=0.85; vs PoisonedRAG ASR@5=0.83/CASR=0.76 and Prompt Injection ASR@5=0.80/CASR=0.77 (Table 1)
MS-MARCO (GPT-4o/Contriever): CPA-RAG ASR@5=0.86, CASR=0.80, matching PoisonedRAG's ASR@5=0.86 but higher CASR (Table 1)
Corpus Poisoning baseline induces target answers poorly: ASR<=0.07 despite high F1 (Table 1)
Cross-model mean ASR@5 ~0.95 (CPA-RAG black-box) vs 0.90 (PoisonedRAG black-box) on NQ across 8 LLMs; HotpotQA ASR@5=1.0 (Table 2)
Concealment: CPA-RAG perplexity=43.01 vs PoisonedRAG(W)=487.77, PoisonedRAG(B)=63.21, natural=49.19; grammar/spelling errors=0.59 vs 6.25 / 3.17 / 1.54 (Table 4)
Ablation: Qwen-only generator ASR@5=0.94 on Qwen but only 0.51 on GPT-4o (poor transfer); CPA-RAG Full mean ASR@5~0.9425, F1=0.95 (Table 6)

Reported ~5 pp ASR advantage over black-box PoisonedRAG across top-k; 14.5 pp advantage over black-box baselines under defenses; TES 16.03%-25.60% higher than black-box and 17.71% over white-box at k=10 (Table 3)
Successfully compromised the commercial RAG system on Alibaba's BaiLian platform (Appendix C)
